channel.title: Digital Assets Morning Wire
channel.description: Daily digital-asset regulatory and market digest. AI-sourced and AI-generated — verify against official sources before acting.

item.title: Coinbase's Jesse Pollak steps back from Base app leadership after admitting his crypto social strategy failed
item.description: Coinbase executive Jesse Pollak stepped back from leading the company's Base app, handing the role to Jordan Fish, known as Cobie, after acknowledging that its social-media-style features failed, with the Base blockchain refocusing on trading, stablecoin payments and artificial-intelligence agents. So what: The reshuffle matters mainly as a signal that a large United States exchange group is steering its blockchain platform toward stablecoin payments and trading, activities with more direct financial-crime-compliance touchpoints than the abandoned social features. [single source]

item.title: ‘Hugely positive’ meeting on Thursday with Trump to discuss ethics raises hopes for passage of sweeping crypto legislation
item.description: Republican senators Bernie Moreno and Cynthia Lummis met President Trump to seek agreement on ethics provisions restricting how federal officials can profit from digital assets, part of the Clarity Act, the sweeping United States crypto market-structure bill that Senate leaders aim to bring to a floor vote before the August recess. So what: Passage of the Clarity Act would redraw the United States regulatory perimeter for digital-asset businesses, so the bill's progress is worth tracking for any compliance function overseeing United States counterparties, though obligations change only if it becomes law. [single source]

item.title: Coinbase’s Jesse Pollak hands Base app leadership to Cobie after admitting social bets fell short
item.description: Jesse Pollak handed leadership of Coinbase's Base app team to Jordan Fish, the Echo founder known as Cobie who joined Coinbase through its roughly 375 million dollar acquisition of Echo, after conceding that the app's social and creator-coin bets fell short. So what: A renewed Coinbase focus on stablecoin payments and tokenised-asset trading could gradually increase the crypto-linked payment flows that bank screening and monitoring systems encounter. [single source]

item.title: BlackRock outlines vision for crypto-TradFi convergence as product pipeline grows
item.description: BlackRock, the world's largest asset manager, used its second-quarter earnings call to set out a strategy for connecting crypto-assets with traditional finance, including plans to tokenise its funds and applications to the United States Securities and Exchange Commission for two tokenised money-market funds that would accept stablecoin subscriptions. So what: When mainstream asset managers plan tokenised funds that accept stablecoin subscriptions, the boundary between conventional securities operations and digital-asset flows narrows, so monitoring and due-diligence frameworks should anticipate tokenised fund units and stablecoin legs appearing in ordinary client activity. [corroborated]

item.title: Kraken Institutional taps Upshift to build vaults that earn yield on idle bitcoin, ETH and stablecoins
item.description: Crypto exchange Kraken's institutional arm is partnering with the on-chain yield platform Upshift to build customised vaults that let institutional clients earn returns on idle bitcoin, ether and stablecoin holdings connected to their Kraken custody accounts. So what: Yield programmes that deploy custodied client assets into on-chain strategies blur the line between custody and investment services, so counterparty assessments of exchanges should establish where client assets actually sit and how the advertised yield is generated. [single source]

item.title: Ostium pauses trading after apparent $18 million vault exploit
item.description: Ostium, a decentralised derivatives-trading platform, halted trading after an attacker manipulated its price-reporting system to steal about $18 million of the stablecoin USD Coin (USDC) from its liquidity vault, then swapped the funds into ether and spread them across multiple wallets. So what: Exploit proceeds of this kind are typically laundered quickly through token swaps and wallet dispersion, so screening teams relying on blockchain analytics should expect addresses linked to this theft to surface in exchange-bound flows over the coming weeks. [single source]

item.title: Ostium suffers $18 million exploit as oracle attack wave continues to hit DeFi
item.description: An attacker exploited the price-oracle system of Ostium, a decentralised derivatives platform, to drain about $18 million of the stablecoin USD Coin (USDC), the latest in a run of oracle-manipulation attacks on decentralised-finance protocols. So what: A sustained wave of oracle-manipulation thefts keeps decentralised-finance protocols a recurring source of stolen funds, reinforcing the case for treating exposure to such protocols, whether direct or through clients, as elevated financial-crime risk. [single source]

item.title: William Blair cuts Coinbase forecasts but says crypto downturn nearing a bottom
item.description: Investment bank William Blair cut its revenue and earnings forecasts for the crypto exchange Coinbase, citing sharply lower expected trading volumes, while keeping a positive rating on the view that the crypto market downturn is close to bottoming out. So what: Analyst commentary of this kind carries little direct compliance impact, though a prolonged downturn in exchange revenues is useful context when reviewing the financial resilience of crypto counterparties in periodic due-diligence cycles. [single source]

item.title: President Trump expected to meet with senators to work on ethics concerns in crypto bill
item.description: The White House is planning a meeting between President Trump and United States senators to try to resolve a stand-off over the ethics provisions of the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act, the stalled bill that would create a market-structure framework for United States crypto markets. So what: The bill's fate will determine the federal rulebook governing digital-asset market activity in the United States, so institutions with United States operations should track whether negotiators clear the ethics dispute before the Senate's August recess narrows the window for passage this year. [single source]

item.title: South Korea to modify 76-year-old law to classify cryptocurrencies as national assets
item.description: South Korea's finance ministry announced plans to revise the country's 1950 state-property law so cryptocurrencies can be classified as national assets, part of a wider programme under which capital-markets amendments recognising blockchain ledgers as securities registries take effect on 4 February 2027. So what: South Korea embedding crypto-assets and blockchain securities registries into public-finance law signals accelerating official adoption of tokenised instruments across Asia-Pacific, relevant context when setting risk appetite for regional digital-asset counterparties and products. [single source]

item.title: Open USD poses biggest threat yet to Circle's USDC, CoinShares says
item.description: Research firm CoinShares said Open USD, a planned institutional stablecoin backed by a consortium of more than 140 companies including BlackRock, Coinbase, Mastercard, Stripe and Visa, poses the most serious competitive threat yet to Circle's USD Coin (USDC) because it would share reserve income with participating businesses. So what: A consortium stablecoin from mainstream payments and asset-management firms, expected in the second half of 2026, could redistribute dollar-token flows across issuers, so stablecoin exposure mapping and issuer due-diligence coverage will need refreshing around its launch. [single source]

item.title: DTCC begins first tokenized stock and Treasury production trades involving JPMorgan, BlackRock and Goldman
item.description: The Depository Trust & Clearing Corporation (DTCC), which runs the central clearing and settlement infrastructure for United States securities, began its first limited production trades of tokenised stocks and Treasury bonds with nearly forty firms including JPMorgan, BlackRock and Goldman Sachs, under a no-action letter from the United States Securities and Exchange Commission. So what: Tokenised versions of mainstream United States securities settling through core market infrastructure mean blockchain-settled instruments will increasingly appear inside ordinary custody and settlement chains, so control frameworks built for crypto-native venues will need extending to tokenised traditional assets. [corroborated]

item.title: Tokenization startup Tradable plans to bring $1 billion worth of private credit assets to Stellar
item.description: Tokenisation platform Tradable announced plans to bring up to $1 billion of private credit assets onto the Stellar blockchain, after previously tokenising about $1.7 billion of private credit positions on another network. So what: The steady migration of private credit onto public blockchains widens the range of tokenised real-world assets that can appear as client holdings or collateral, making familiarity with their issuance and transfer mechanics part of routine due diligence. [single source]

item.title: How Crypto Venues Are Building Financial Operating Systems: Binance as a Super App
item.description: A research report published by The Block and commissioned by Binance describes how the exchange has grown into a multi-asset financial platform, spanning trading, payments and tokenised stock products, serving more than 320 million registered users. So what: Exchanges evolving into broad financial platforms concentrate large volumes of customer activity and assets in single venues, raising the materiality of counterparty due diligence and exposure monitoring wherever bank relationships touch those platforms. [single source]

item.title: Circle suspended Tether-backed fund over market manipulation concerns, arbitration filings show
item.description: Newly disclosed arbitration filings show stablecoin issuer Circle suspended the account of Heka Funds, a Malta-based fund financed mostly by rival issuer Tether, over concerns its trading could amount to market manipulation, a decision an arbitrator upheld in February 2026. So what: The case shows a stablecoin issuer using account-suspension powers over suspected market abuse and a fund omitting its dominant backer at onboarding, offering concrete lessons for beneficial-ownership verification and for expectations of issuer counterparties' own controls. [single source]

item.title: BlackRock's crypto assets fall 39% despite $15 billion of net inflows
item.description: BlackRock reported that assets in its digital-asset funds fell 39 percent over the past year to about $48.8 billion, as roughly $45.8 billion of market depreciation from falling crypto prices outweighed $15.1 billion of net investor inflows. So what: Falling crypto fund values driven by prices rather than withdrawals are chiefly a market-risk story, but they are useful background when interpreting shifts in clients' digital-asset transaction patterns during the downturn. [single source]

item.title: Japan reclassifies crypto as a financial asset, paves way for tax cuts
item.description: Japan's parliament passed amendments to the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act and the Payment Services Act that reclassify cryptoassets as financial instruments rather than payment tools, adding insider-trading rules and heavier penalties for unregistered operators; a separate law enacted earlier in 2026 sets a flat 20 percent tax on crypto gains starting in 2028. So what: Japan's shift to securities-style regulation of cryptoassets, with the new framework due to take effect in 2027, will change disclosure and market-conduct expectations for Japanese exchanges and counterparties that Asia-Pacific-focused compliance teams deal with. [corroborated]

item.title: AI agentic payments enter mainstream as Visa, Mastercard, Ripple back x402 standard
item.description: Visa, Mastercard, American Express, Ripple, Google and about forty other companies joined a Linux Foundation body governing x402, an open payments standard originally built by Coinbase that lets artificial-intelligence software agents make small stablecoin payments to online services without holding accounts. So what: Payments initiated by autonomous software agents with no account relationship sit awkwardly with customer due-diligence and transaction-monitoring models built around identifiable customers, so the standard's adoption by major card networks is worth tracking when assessing stablecoin payment flows. [single source]

item.title: Website alert: trustwallet[.]uk[.]com
item.description: The Hong Kong Monetary Authority warned the public that the website trustwallet[.]uk[.]com refers to a 'Trust Wallet' service that holds no licence under Hong Kong's Payment Systems and Stored Value Facilities Ordinance to issue, or facilitate the issue of, stored-value facilities in Hong Kong. So what: The flagged domain can be added to fraud and phishing watchlists, and the alert is a reminder that clients in Hong Kong may be targeted by unlicensed wallet services impersonating well-known crypto brands. [official]

item.title: JPMorgan says bitcoin outlook sees ‘encouraging sign’ as Strategy boosts cash reserves
item.description: JPMorgan analysts said strengthening activity in bitcoin futures points to renewed institutional interest, while the corporate bitcoin holder Strategy lifted its cash reserves to 3 billion United States dollars so it can pay preferred dividends without being forced to sell bitcoin. So what: This is market commentary rather than regulatory news; its relevance is limited to background awareness of institutional bitcoin sentiment when reviewing client and counterparty digital-asset exposure. [single source]

item.title: ‘We’re going to get it done’: Crypto bill faces what could be final test over Trump conflicts of interest
item.description: United States Senate negotiators are still working through conflict-of-interest provisions covering officials including President Trump that stand between the Clarity Act — a bill setting out which federal agencies would regulate digital-asset markets — and a Senate floor vote targeted before the August recess. So what: If enacted, the Clarity Act would settle which United States regulators oversee digital-asset trading and custody, a structural change that would reshape the rulebooks United States exchange and custody counterparties operate under. [single source]

item.title: $1.9 trillion asset manager T. Rowe Price bets on active management with first multi-token crypto ETF
item.description: United States asset manager T. Rowe Price launched the industry's first actively managed exchange-traded fund holding a basket of spot cryptoassets, trading under the ticker TKNZ, with portfolio managers adjusting allocations across bitcoin, ether and several other tokens. So what: Actively managed multi-token funds from mainstream asset managers extend regulated, exchange-listed crypto exposure to conventional portfolios, widening the range of clients whose digital-asset holdings sit inside familiar securities-account channels. [single source]

item.title: Visa backs Open USD with new stablecoin platform as Circle faces fresh competition
item.description: Visa launched a stablecoin platform that lets banks and other financial institutions issue, store, transfer and redeem stablecoins across its payment network, starting with Open USD, a new stablecoin from a consortium whose backers include Visa, BlackRock, Alphabet and Coinbase. So what: Card-network infrastructure that puts stablecoin issuance and redemption inside regulated banks brings corresponding anti-money-laundering, sanctions-screening and reserve-oversight duties, making this a concrete reference point for how bank stablecoin services are likely to be structured. [corroborated]

item.title: $1.9 trillion asset manager T. Rowe Price launches first actively managed multi-token crypto ETF
item.description: T. Rowe Price began trading the T. Rowe Price Crypto ETF, described as the first actively managed multi-token spot crypto exchange-traded fund, on the New York Stock Exchange's Arca market with about 15 million United States dollars in assets weighted mainly to bitcoin and ether. So what: A mainstream asset manager running an actively managed basket of cryptoassets in an exchange-traded wrapper broadens the routes through which bank clients can hold digital-asset exposure inside ordinary securities accounts. [single source]

item.title: Visa launching internal stablecoin platform for clients that provides access to OUSD: report
item.description: Fortune reported that Visa is building a platform for its bank and merchant clients to mint, redeem and use Open USD (OUSD), a stablecoin from the Open Standard consortium of more than 140 companies, alongside Visa's existing support for Circle's USD Coin and Paxos's Global Dollar. So what: Free, unlimited minting and redemption of a consortium stablecoin through a card network would concentrate significant payment flows in one instrument, raising questions about reserve quality, redemption controls and screening coverage that compliance functions will want answered before such rails are used. [corroborated]

item.title: US jury indicts South Dakota crypto investor on 29 counts over alleged $20 million investor fraud
item.description: A United States federal grand jury indicted South Dakota investor Benjamin Wiener on 29 counts including wire fraud, money laundering, bank fraud and aggravated identity theft over an alleged 20 million dollar Ponzi-style scheme that took cash and digital currency from dozens of investors and moved it through banks and crypto exchanges to hide its origin. So what: The alleged laundering pattern — investor funds layered through bank accounts and crypto exchanges before personal spending — is a useful typology for transaction-monitoring scenarios covering accounts linked to informal investment schemes. [single source]

item.title: Crypto brokerage firm Alpaca raises $135 million for tokenized stock infrastructure
item.description: Crypto brokerage Alpaca raised 135 million United States dollars in equity, with debt financing lifting the total package to 435 million, to expand the infrastructure behind tokenised United States equities, a market in which it says it clears or custodies about 94 percent of products. So what: The concentration of most tokenised United States equity clearing and custody in a single infrastructure provider is a counterparty-concentration point worth noting when assessing exposure to tokenised-security products. [single source]

item.title: A crypto risk management framework for financial institutions
item.description: Blockchain analytics firm Elliptic published a risk-management framework arguing that financial institutions can cover cryptoasset exposure within their existing compliance programmes by mapping it onto customer, geographic, product and on-chain behavioural risk categories aligned with Financial Action Task Force (FATF) and Wolfsberg Group standards. So what: The framework is a usable benchmark for the live design question of whether digital-asset risk should sit inside the existing anti-money-laundering risk assessment or in a stand-alone programme. [single source]

item.title: Tether invests $20 million in Ualá as the fintech firm reaches $3.2 billion valuation
item.description: Stablecoin issuer Tether invested 20 million United States dollars in Ualá, an Argentine digital bank with more than 11 million customers and banking licences in Argentina, Mexico and Colombia, as part of a funding round valuing the firm at 3.2 billion dollars. So what: Stablecoin issuers taking stakes in licensed retail banking platforms in Latin America point to deeper links between regulated deposit-taking and stablecoin distribution, useful background when reviewing correspondent or fintech relationships with exposure to those markets. [single source]

item.title: Jeff Booth, Lyn Alden-backed ORANGE JUICE raises $40 million to launch permanent capital firm with bitcoin treasury strategy
item.description: Investors including Jeff Booth and Lyn Alden launched ORANGE JUICE, a Connecticut-based permanent capital firm that raised 40 million US dollars to buy small American businesses and reinvest their cash flow into bitcoin holdings. So what: The spread of investment vehicles that convert operating cash flow into bitcoin holdings is context for source-of-wealth and treasury-exposure assessments when such firms appear as clients or counterparties. [single source]

item.title: Tether invests $20 million in Argentine neobank Ualá as it expands Latin America push
item.description: Stablecoin issuer Tether invested 20 million US dollars for a stake of roughly 0.6 percent in Uala, an Argentine digital bank with more than 11 million customers, as part of a wider expansion across Latin America. So what: A stablecoin issuer taking equity stakes in regulated retail banks means ownership-structure and group-risk reviews of banking counterparties may increasingly need to assess stablecoin-issuer shareholders and their reserve transparency. [single source]

item.title: U.S. adds four Iran central bank crypto wallets to sanctions, Tether freezes $131 million of contents
item.description: The United States Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) added four cryptocurrency wallet addresses linked to the Central Bank of Iran to its sanctions list, and stablecoin issuer Tether froze 131 million US dollars held in them. So what: Sanctions-screening systems should pick up the newly designated wallet addresses promptly, and exposure reviews should extend beyond direct dealings to indirect flows through the liquidity providers and payment processors that serviced the wallets. [corroborated]

item.title: Visa says stablecoins will power micro-commerce in AI agentic economy
item.description: Payments network Visa, with analytics firm Artemis, published a research report arguing that stablecoins will handle very small machine-to-machine payments in an economy of artificial-intelligence agents, while card networks keep larger consumer purchases. So what: The report flags that existing legal and dispute-resolution frameworks were not designed for payments delegated to artificial-intelligence agents, an emerging control gap to watch as stablecoin-based payment products reach mainstream bank customers. [single source]

item.title: Ether outruns bitcoin as ETF money returns, almost all of from BlackRock's fund
item.description: Exchange-traded funds holding ether drew 96 million US dollars of inflows over three days, almost all into BlackRock's products, while bitcoin funds swung between large daily outflows and inflows. So what: No direct compliance action follows from these fund flows, though the concentration of institutional demand in a single asset manager's exchange-traded products is useful context for counterparty and market-risk monitoring. [single source]

item.title: OFAC Sanctions Iran Central Bank Crypto Wallets, Freezing $131M in Stablecoins
item.description: Blockchain analytics firm Chainalysis published an analysis of the United States Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) designation of four Central Bank of Iran cryptocurrency wallets, tracing how the wallets received funds before issuer Tether froze 131 million US dollars of its stablecoin. So what: The tracing links the sanctioned wallets to an institutional liquidity provider and an Asia-based payment processor, reinforcing that sanctions-exposure reviews should cover indirect relationships with such intermediaries as well as the listed addresses themselves. [corroborated]

item.title: Upbit parent Dunamu faces sanction process nearly eight months after $30 million hack: report
item.description: South Korea's Financial Supervisory Service has taken the first formal step in a sanction process against Dunamu, operator of the cryptocurrency exchange Upbit, nearly eight months after roughly 30 million US dollars of assets were stolen from the exchange. So what: The case tests how South Korea's virtual-asset user-protection law applies to exchange security failures, a precedent relevant to due diligence on exchange counterparties and to incident-accountability expectations across Asian markets. [single source]

item.title: The GENIUS Act turns 1: State of Crypto
item.description: One year after the United States enacted the GENIUS Act, its first federal stablecoin law, banking regulators are still developing the implementing rules, with proposals covering custody, capital, liquidity and customer due-diligence checks for stablecoin issuers out for comment but none yet final. So what: The direction of United States stablecoin rulemaking, including proposed know-your-customer obligations on issuers, will shape the regulatory standing of the stablecoins most used by clients and counterparties in Asia, so the finalisation timetable is worth tracking. [single source]

item.title: Bitcoin's biggest advocate, Michael Saylor, says new plan to clean up the blockchain is 'a bad idea'
item.description: Michael Saylor, a prominent bitcoin advocate, publicly opposed Bitcoin Improvement Proposal 110, a proposed one-year change to the network's rules that would restrict storing arbitrary data on the bitcoin blockchain, arguing it threatens the network's neutrality. So what: The debate carries no direct regulatory implication, but a contested change to bitcoin's core rules can create network-split risk that would complicate custody, settlement and asset-listing decisions across the industry. [single source]

item.title: Tether's USDT hits 2-year countdown threatening its position on U.S. crypto platforms
item.description: Analysis of the United States GENIUS Act stablecoin law indicates Tether has until around July 2028 to restructure its stablecoin's reserves into cash and Treasury bills and register with the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency, or United States platforms will have to stop offering it. So what: If the largest stablecoin cannot satisfy United States requirements by 2028, liquidity and counterparty exposure could shift across venues worldwide, so the issuer's reserve restructuring and registration progress belongs in ongoing stablecoin risk assessments. [single source]

item.title: Kraken says simpler options can unlock crypto's next derivatives market
item.description: Cryptocurrency exchange Kraken launched cash-settled bitcoin and ether options priced in US dollars on its professional platform for eligible international clients, saying simpler product design can broaden the crypto derivatives market. So what: Broader retail access to crypto derivatives at global exchanges is relevant context for exchange counterparty reviews and for anticipating how regulators in Hong Kong and other Asian markets approach suitability and conduct standards for such products. [single source]

item.title: Inside Zcash's new node that targets Visa-scale privacy at 50,000 transactions per second
item.description: Independent developers released Zakura, new software for running the privacy-focused Zcash network, as part of an effort to scale private transactions from roughly one per second today toward 50,000 per second. So what: Greater scalability for privacy-preserving cryptocurrencies is relevant to transaction-monitoring and asset-listing policies, since shielded transfers limit the tracing on which anti-money-laundering controls rely. [single source]

item.title: US regulators miss GENIUS Act’s one-year deadline for final stablecoin rules
item.description: United States regulators missed the Guiding and Establishing National Innovation for U.S. Stablecoins (GENIUS) Act's one-year deadline to finalise federal stablecoin rules, leaving reserve, capital, custody, customer-identification and anti-money-laundering rule packages at the proposal stage while the law's January 2027 effective date stands. So what: Stablecoin-exposed compliance programmes face a compressed adjustment window, as final United States reserve, custody and anti-money-laundering requirements may now arrive only shortly before the law's January 2027 effective date. [single source]

item.title: Bank of America taps new leaders to bridge crypto, AI and traditional finance
item.description: Bank of America appointed Sonali Theisen to lead its global digital-assets platform, covering tokenised deposits, stablecoins, digital collateral, trading settlement and custody, alongside a new head of artificial-intelligence transformation. So what: Digital-asset platform build-outs at major United States banks signal that tokenised deposits and stablecoin services are moving into mainstream banking, so correspondent and counterparty reviews will increasingly need to understand peer institutions' cryptoasset activities. [single source]

item.title: CryptoQuant says Strategy still needs disciplined bitcoin buying and selling framework
item.description: Research firm CryptoQuant said Strategy, the largest corporate holder of bitcoin, still lacks a systematic model for timing bitcoin purchases and a disciplined framework for selling, despite the capital-management framework the company announced in late June. So what: Concentrated corporate bitcoin treasuries carry liquidity and governance risks that remain relevant when risk-assessing clients or counterparties whose balance sheets depend heavily on cryptoasset holdings. [single source]

item.title: Stripe and Swift race to control the next generation of global payments infrastructure
item.description: Payments firm Stripe has launched an unsolicited 53 billion United States dollar bid for PayPal, whose platform includes a dollar stablecoin, while the interbank messaging network Swift is expanding a blockchain-based settlement initiative to more than 40 financial institutions. So what: Consolidation of stablecoin and blockchain settlement capability inside the largest payment networks would concentrate transaction flows that sanctions-screening and transaction-monitoring arrangements at correspondent banks will need to cover. [single source]

item.title: Bitcoin slides toward $63,000 as Coinbase premium stays negative for a record 60 days
item.description: Bitcoin fell below 63,000 United States dollars as the Coinbase price premium, a gauge of United States institutional demand, stayed negative for a record 60 consecutive days. So what: This is market context rather than a control trigger, though a prolonged downturn is relevant when assessing the financial resilience of virtual-asset trading platforms and other crypto-exposed clients. [single source]

item.title: Bitcoin ETFs could mirror gold’s history of ‘spectacular gains’ and ‘painful drawdowns,’ analyst says
item.description: A Bloomberg exchange-traded-fund analyst argued that bitcoin exchange-traded funds may repeat gold funds' pattern of spectacular gains followed by long drawdowns, with the largest bitcoin fund's assets down to about 60 billion United States dollars from a brief 100 billion peak. So what: Fund-flow volatility is primarily an investor-suitability matter, though the continued shift of retail cryptoasset exposure into regulated fund wrappers moves activity into channels with established intermediary oversight. [single source]

item.title: Bitcoin faces fresh headwinds as China’s Kimi beats Claude, GPT in coding benchmark
item.description: Bitcoin came under pressure after Beijing-based Moonshot AI released an open-weight model that topped United States rivals on a coding benchmark, unsettling the artificial-intelligence data-centre business model that many bitcoin miners have adopted. So what: There is no regulatory action here; the episode mainly illustrates how closely cryptoasset prices now track the artificial-intelligence investment cycle, a market linkage rather than a financial-crime-compliance issue. [single source]

item.title: Airbnb CEO says X account was hacked, attacker posted AI-slop on tokenization
item.description: Airbnb's chief executive said his account on the social-media platform X was hacked, with the attacker posting artificial-intelligence-generated promotional threads about tokenising real-world assets, though no scam links or wallet addresses were posted. So what: Compromised high-profile social-media accounts remain a standard opening move in cryptoasset investment scams, reinforcing customer-education and fraud-typology material warning that authentic-looking endorsements of token products can be fake. [single source]

item.title: Taiwan sentences BitShine crypto exchange ringleader to 22 years in $39 million fraud case: reports
item.description: A Taiwanese court sentenced the ringleader of the BitShine cryptocurrency-exchange fraud to 22 years in prison for defrauding about 1,539 victims of roughly 39 million United States dollars and laundering larger sums overseas via the stablecoin Tether. So what: The group hired compliance staff to build legitimate-looking customer checks and then coached members to bypass them, underlining that polished onboarding paperwork at regional virtual-asset platforms is not reliable evidence of genuine anti-money-laundering controls. [corroborated]

item.title: UK gang jailed over $5.4 million crypto scam posing as police
item.description: A London court jailed three men who posed as police officers and used fake police websites to steal about 4 million pounds (5.4 million United States dollars) in cryptocurrency from eight victims, following a Metropolitan Police investigation. So what: Police-impersonation scams that trick victims into surrendering cryptocurrency credentials are an active typology, and customer transfers prompted by purported law-enforcement contact remain a red flag worth reflecting in fraud and transaction-monitoring scenarios. [corroborated]

item.title: USDT vs USDC: Comparing the Two Largest Stablecoins
item.description: An educational explainer compares the two largest dollar-pegged stablecoins, Tether (USDT) and USD Coin (USDC), on market share, reserves, transparency and regulatory standing, noting USD Coin complies with the European Union's Markets in Crypto-Assets framework while Tether does not meet new United States stablecoin-law requirements. So what: Reserve transparency and regulatory standing differ materially between the two dominant stablecoins, which matters when risk-rating client flows, since tokens restricted in some markets can carry heightened regulatory and counterparty risk. [single source]

item.title: Grayscale could take Worldcoin to Wall Street after ETF filing with the SEC
item.description: Asset manager Grayscale filed a registration statement with the United States Securities and Exchange Commission for an exchange-traded fund that would hold Worldcoin, the token of the eye-scanning digital-identity project co-founded by OpenAI's chief executive. So what: The steady extension of United States exchange-traded funds to smaller, novel tokens widens the set of cryptoassets reachable through mainstream brokerage channels, gradually expanding what product-approval and screening processes may need to cover. [single source]

item.title: Trump’s crypto ethics stance leaves Clarity Act negotiations in limbo
item.description: Negotiations over the Clarity Act, the proposed United States law that would set a market-structure framework for cryptoassets, remain stalled because President Trump has not agreed to ethics language restricting federal officials' profits from cryptoassets, with a Senate vote targeted for early August. So what: Delay to the main United States market-structure bill prolongs regulatory uncertainty for cryptoasset counterparties with United States exposure, leaving current rules as the operative baseline for counterparty and product assessments ahead of a possible Senate vote in early August. [single source]

item.title: Vietnam sets new crypto trading fines on par with drunk-driving penalties
item.description: Vietnam's government issued a decree, taking effect on 1 September 2026, that fines individuals and organisations for trading cryptoassets outside newly licensed domestic exchanges, with penalties for unlicensed service providers of up to about 200 million Vietnamese dong (roughly 7,600 United States dollars). So what: Vietnam is drawing a hard licensing perimeter around cryptoasset trading, so country-risk assessments and transaction-monitoring assumptions for Vietnamese retail and corporate flows to offshore exchanges will need updating once the penalties take effect in September. [corroborated]

item.title: Where crypto fits in the FATF’s new public-private partnership report
item.description: Analytics firm Elliptic reviewed a new Financial Action Task Force (FATF) report on public-private partnerships against financial crime, which finds the cryptoasset industry remains under-integrated into government-industry information-sharing arrangements. So what: With the Financial Action Task Force pressing for deeper public-private information sharing under the new United Kingdom presidency, banks active in digital assets can expect encouragement to bring cryptoasset expertise and blockchain analytics into existing intelligence-sharing partnerships, including in Hong Kong. [single source]

item.title: Saylor's Strategy raises cash reserves to $3.2 billion, leaving bitcoin holdings unchanged
item.description: Strategy, the largest corporate holder of bitcoin, raised its cash reserves to about 3.2 billion United States dollars through share sales while leaving its roughly 843,775 bitcoin untouched, rebuilding a buffer to support dividends on its preferred stock after recent market pressure on its financing model. So what: The episode illustrates how leveraged corporate bitcoin-treasury structures can come under funding strain in downturns, a concentration and credit consideration when assessing exposure to counterparties whose balance sheets are dominated by cryptoassets. [single source]

item.title: Brazil’s securities regulator sets up task force with 60-day deadline for tokenization proposal
item.description: Brazil's securities regulator established a working group, due to report within 60 days of its installation, to propose an experimental framework for issuing, trading and settling tokenised securities, covering questions such as official ownership records, private-key custody and transaction reversibility. So what: Another sizeable market designing rules for tokenised securities adds to the fragmented international landscape that compliance functions supporting custody and tokenisation business must track, and Brazil's answers on private-key custody and transaction reversibility may be cited in policy debates elsewhere. [single source]

item.title: Ethereum co-founder Vitalik Buterin ‘vibe-codes’ anonymous billboard demo with onchain moderation on Aztec
item.description: Ethereum co-founder Vitalik Buterin released a demonstration application on the privacy-focused Aztec network that lets users post messages with no public link to their identity or original deposits, moderated by an automated system whose rules are stored transparently on the blockchain. So what: Privacy systems that sever the link between deposits and subsequent on-chain activity illustrate the limits of blockchain-tracing tools, a factor to weigh when risk-rating exposure to privacy-focused networks in transaction monitoring and source-of-funds reviews. [single source]

item.title: Cross-chain protocol Allbridge halts after $1.65 million flash loan exploit
item.description: Cross-chain bridge protocol Allbridge paused operations after an attacker used a flash loan — a large uncollateralised loan borrowed and repaid within a single transaction — to distort its Solana stablecoin pools and extract about 1.65 million United States dollars. So what: The stolen funds were quickly bridged to Ethereum and dispersed across addresses, a reminder that exploit proceeds move fast across chains and that exposure screening should pick up addresses linked to newly reported bridge incidents. [single source]

item.title: Bitcoin ETFs see new money again, but inflows remain ‘peanuts’ relative to the recent exodus
item.description: United States-listed spot bitcoin exchange-traded funds recorded about 273 million United States dollars of net inflows over two weeks, ending an eight-week withdrawal streak in which more than 8 billion United States dollars had left the products. So what: Fund-flow swings of this scale mainly signal shifting institutional sentiment rather than any compliance obligation, though sustained outflows from exchange-traded products can foreshadow reduced client activity in related brokerage and custody services. [single source]

item.title: Allbridge Core pauses protocol after $1.65 million flash loan exploit: onchain analysts
item.description: Cross-chain bridge protocol Allbridge Core was paused after a flash-loan attack manipulated its stablecoin pools for a loss of about 1.65 million United States dollars, with security firms tracking the stolen funds as they moved through privacy tools from Solana to Ethereum. So what: The routing of bridge-exploit proceeds through privacy protocols within hours underlines why screening tools need timely incident-attribution data, particularly for funds arriving from cross-chain bridges. [single source]

item.title: Stablecoins vs Traditional Banking
item.description: An educational explainer contrasts stablecoins with traditional bank deposits, noting that under the United States' GENIUS Act stablecoin issuers must hold full reserves and may not pay interest to holders, while banks retain deposit insurance, lending functions and newly chartered stablecoin competitors under federal supervision. So what: The piece is background reading rather than a new development, but the distinction it draws — full-reserve, non-interest-bearing stablecoins versus insured bank deposits — frames how client questions about stablecoin exposure are likely to arise as United States-regulated issuers expand. [single source]

item.title: Saylor urges Bitcoin to reject BIP-110 in 110-point essay as soft fork’s August showdown approaches
item.description: Michael Saylor, co-founder of bitcoin-holding company Strategy, published an essay opposing Bitcoin Improvement Proposal 110 (BIP-110), a contested temporary change to Bitcoin's rules that would restrict data-heavy transactions, ahead of a miner signalling period expected to begin in early August. So what: A contested change to Bitcoin's consensus rules carries some risk of a network split, an operational scenario that custody and settlement functions typically prepare for with deposit-freeze and fork-handling procedures. [single source]
